aWeek 5
1. Complex Numbers 2. Complex Matrices 3. Hermitian Matrix 4. Unitary Matrix 5. Block Matrix Multiplication 6. Schur's Theorem 7. Spectral Theorem
1. Complex NumbersA complex number is of the form a+iba+ib, where a,b∈Ra,b∈R and i=-1i=-1. The set of all the complex number is given by:C={a+ib|a,b∈R}C={a+ib|a,b∈R} In a+iba+ib, aa is referred to as the "real part" and bb is referred to as the "imaginary part". We can plot (a,b)(a,b) in a complex plane. A complex plan has real axis and an imaginary axis. The horizontal axis is the real axis and the vertical axis is the imaginary axis. Let us plot 2+3i2+3i. The point (2,3)(2,3) corresponds to 2+3i2+3i.
ImaginaryAxisRealAxis2+3i2+3i Let z=a+ibz=a+ib. If b=0b=0, then zz is a real number. All real numbers are also complex numplex numbers. The set RR is a proper subset of CC. Addition(a+ib)+(c+id)(a+ib)+(c+id) is given by:
(a+ib)+(c+id)=(a+c)+i(b+d)
(a+ib)+(c+id)=(a+c)+i(b+d)Multiplication(a+ib)×(c+id)(a+ib)×(c+id) is given by:(a+ib)×(c+id)=(ac-bd)+i(ad+bc)(a+ib)×(c+id)=(ac-bd)+i(ad+bc) ConjugateLet z=a+ibz=a+ib. Then, conjugate of zz, denoted by ⏨zz¯, is given by⏨⏨⏨a+ib=a-iba+ib¯=a-ib Geometrically, the coordinates of the conjugate is a reflection along the real axis.
2+3i2+3i2-3i2-3iImaginaryAxisRealAxis Let z=a+ibz=a+ib, and ⏨z=a-ibz¯=a-ib. If z=⏨zz=z¯, then zz is a real number.
z=⏨z
a+ib=a-ib

b=0
z=z¯a+ib=a-ib2ib=0b=0Since b=0b=0, a+iba+ib is a real number. Absolute ValueLet z=a+ibz=a+ib. The absolute value of zz, denoted by |z||z|, is given by
|a+ib|=(a+ib)(⏨⏨⏨a+ib)
=(a+ib)(a-ib)
=a2+b2
|a+ib|=(a+ib)(a+ib¯)=(a+ib)(a-ib)=a2+b2Notice that a2+b2a2+b2 represents the length of the vector (a,b)(a,b) in the complex plane. The square of the absolute value is given by|z|2=z×⏨z|z|2=z×z¯ The aboslute value is always greater than or equal to 00. This is because, it represents the length.DivisionLet z1=a+ibz1=a+ib and z2=c+idz2=c+id.

z1z2=z1×z2¯z2×z2¯=(a+ib)×(c-id)(c+id)×(c-id)=(ac-bd)+i(ad+bc)c2+d2=(ac-bdc2+d2)+i(ad+bcc2+d2) Polar CoordinatesIn the cartesian coordinate system, a point is represented by its xx and yy coordinates : (x,y):(x,y). In the polar coordinate system, a point is represented by its length and its counter clockwise angle with the horizontal axis. Let us focus on two points. • (1,1)(1,1)○ Length :: 22○ Counter clockwise angle with the horizontal axis :: 45°45°○ Polar Coordinate :: (2,45°)(2,45°)• (-2,2)(-2,2)○ Length :: 88○ Counter clockwise angle with the horizontal axis :: 135°135°○ Polar Coordinate :: (8,135°)(8,135°)
(2,45°)(2,45°)(8,135°)(8,135°)45°45°135°135° The second coordinate can also be the negative of the clockwise angle with the horizontal axis. For (1,1)(1,1), the clockwise angle is 315°315°. The point (1,1)(1,1) can also be expressed as (2,-315°)(2,-315°). Let us focus on z=a+ibz=a+ib and ⏨z=a-ibz¯=a-ib, where a,b∈Ra,b∈R. Let the length of both the points be rr, where
r=||z||=||⏨z||=a2+b2
r=|z|=|z¯|=a2+b2 Recall, for a right angled triangle, with angle 𝜃°𝜃°,cos(𝜃°)=

This is similar to what we have seen for real matrices PP and QQ where, (PQ)T=QTPT(PQ)T=QTPT• v*(𝛼u+𝛽w)=𝛼(v*u)+𝛽(v*w)v*(𝛼u+𝛽w)=𝛼(v*u)+𝛽(v*w)• ((𝛽v)*)(u+w)=⏨𝛽(v*u+v*w)((𝛽v)*)(u+w)=𝛽¯(v*u+v*w) Standard Inner ProductLet uu and vv be two complex vectors. The standar inner product of uu and vv is given as follows.⟨u,v⟩=u*v⟨u,v⟩=u*v Note : u*vu*v need not be equal to ⟨v,u⟩⟨v,u⟩. Let u=a

3. Hermitian MatrixA complex square matrix AA is a hermitian matrix if A*=AA*=A. ExampleA=a
13+4i
3-4i-5
, A*=a
13+4i
3-4i-5
A=[13+4i3-4i-5],A*=[13+4i3-4i-5] • The diagonal elements of a hermitian matrix are always real numbers. Property 1 : The eigenvalues of a hermitian matrix are real. Proof : Let A=A*A=A* and let Av=𝜆vAv=𝜆v, where v≠0v≠0.
𝜆v*v=v*(𝜆v)
𝜆v*v=v*(Av)
𝜆v*v=v*A*v
𝜆v*v=(Av)*v
𝜆v*v=(𝜆v)*v
𝜆v*v=⏨𝜆(v*v)
𝜆v*v-⏨𝜆v*v=0
(𝜆-⏨𝜆)(v*v)=0
𝜆=⏨𝜆
𝜆v*v=v*(𝜆v)𝜆v*v=v*(Av)𝜆v*v=v*A*v𝜆v*v=(Av)*v𝜆v*v=(𝜆v)*v𝜆v*v=𝜆¯(v*v)𝜆v*v-𝜆¯v*v=0(𝜆-𝜆¯)(v*v)=0𝜆=𝜆¯Since vv is an eigenvector, v≠0v≠0. Therefore, v*v≠0v*v≠0. Since 𝜆=⏨𝜆𝜆=𝜆¯, we can say that 𝜆𝜆 is a real number. Property 2 : The eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a hermitian matrix are orthogonal. Proof : Let A*=AA*=A, Av=𝜆1vAv=𝜆1v and Au=𝜆2uAu=𝜆2u , where v≠0v≠0 and u≠0u≠0 and let 𝜆1≠𝜆2𝜆1≠𝜆2.
Av=𝜆1v
u*Av=𝜆1(u*v)
u*A*v=𝜆1(u*v)
(Au)*v=𝜆1(u*v)
(𝜆2u)*v=𝜆1(u*v)
⏨⏨𝜆2(u*v)=𝜆1(u*v)
𝜆2(u*v)=𝜆1(u*v)
(𝜆2-𝜆1)(u*v)=0
u*v=0
Av=𝜆1vu*Av=𝜆1(u*v)u*A*v=𝜆1(u*v)(Au)*v=𝜆1(u*v)(𝜆2u)*v=𝜆1(u*v)𝜆2¯(u*v)=𝜆1(u*v)𝜆2(u*v)=𝜆1(u*v)(𝜆2-𝜆1)(u*v)=0u*v=0The eigenvalues of a hermitian matrix are real numbers. Therefore, ⏨⏨𝜆2=𝜆2𝜆2¯=𝜆2. Since 𝜆1≠𝜆2𝜆1≠𝜆2, 𝜆2-𝜆1≠0𝜆2-𝜆1≠0. Real Symmetric MatricesA real symmetric matrix is a hermitian matrix. Therefore,• All the eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are real.• The eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are orthogonal
4. Unitary MatrixA complex square matrix UU is unitary if U*U=I=UU*U*U=I=UU*.• The rows of a unitary matrix form an orthonormal set.• The columns of a unitary matrix form an orthonormal set. In fact, for a square matirx, if one of the above two bullet points hold true, then that matrix is a unitary matrix. ExampleU=a
1/2i/2
i/21/2
U=[1/2i/2i/21/2] Let us denote the first and second columns as c1c1 and c2c2. The length of c1c1 and c2c2 are given as follows:
c*1c1=a
1/2-i/2
×a

i/2
c*2c2=a

i/2
1/2
=(1/2)2+(1/2)2 =(1/2)2+(1/2)2
=(1/2)+(1/2) =(1/2)+(1/2)
=1 =1
=1 =1
c1*c1=[1/2-i/2]×[1/2i/2]c2*c2=[-i/21/2]×[i/21/2]=(1/2)2+(1/2)2=(1/2)2+(1/2)2=(1/2)+(1/2)=(1/2)+(1/2)=1=1=1=1 The inner product of c1c1 and c2c2 is given as
c*1c2=a
1/2-i/2

i/2
1/2
=(i/2)+(-i/2)

c1*c2=[1/2-i/2]×[i/21/2]=(i/2)+(-i/2)=0Therefore, UU is a unitary matrix. Property 1 : If Un×nUn×n is a unitary matrix, then ||Uv||2=||v||2||Uv||2=||v||2 for all v∈Cnv∈Cn. Proof :
||Uv||2=(Uv)*(Uv)
=v*U*Uv
=v*Iv
=v*v
=||v||2
||Uv||2=(Uv)*(Uv)=v*U*Uv=v*Iv=v*v=||v||2When a vector is multiplied with a unitiary matrix, the length is unchanged. Property 2 : The absolute value of all the eigenvalues of a unitary matrix is 11 Proof : Let UU be a unitary matrix and let Uv=𝜆vUv=𝜆v, where v≠0v≠0.
||Uv||2=||v||2
(Uv)*(Uv)=v*v
(𝜆v)*(𝜆v)=v*v
(⏨𝜆𝜆)(v*v)=v*v
(⏨𝜆𝜆-1)(v*v)=0
⏨𝜆𝜆-1=0
⏨𝜆𝜆=1
|𝜆|2=1
|𝜆|=1
||Uv||2=||v||2(Uv)*(Uv)=v*v(𝜆v)*(𝜆v)=v*v(𝜆¯𝜆)(v*v)=v*v(𝜆¯𝜆-1)(v*v)=0𝜆¯𝜆-1=0𝜆¯𝜆=1|𝜆|2=1|𝜆|=1Since v≠0v≠0, v*v≠0v*v≠0. The absolute value of a complex number is always greater than or equal to 00, therefore, |𝜆|=1|𝜆|=1. Note : The aboslute value being equal to 11 doesn't imply that all the eigenvalues of a unitary matrix are either 11 or -1-1. For example 𝜆=1
2-i
2𝜆=12-i2 can be an eigenvalue of a unitary matrix.
|𝜆|=𝜆×⏨𝜆=(1
2-i

2)=1=1
|𝜆|=𝜆×𝜆¯=(12-i2)×(12+i2)=(12)+(i2)-(i2)+(12)=1=1 Property 3 : The eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a unitary matrix are orthogonal. Proof : Let Un×nUn×n be a unitary matrix. Let Uv=𝜆1vUv=𝜆1v and Uw=𝜆2wUw=𝜆2w, where v≠0v≠0, w≠0w≠0 and 𝜆1≠𝜆2𝜆1≠𝜆2.
v*w=v*Iw
v*w=v*U*Uw
v*w=(Uv)*(Uw)
v*w=(𝜆1v)*(𝜆2w)
v*w=(⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2)(v*w)
(⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1)(v*w)=0
v*w=v*Iwv*w=v*U*Uwv*w=(Uv)*(Uw)v*w=(𝜆1v)*(𝜆2w)v*w=(𝜆1¯𝜆2)(v*w)(𝜆1¯𝜆2-1)(v*w)=0If we can show that ⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1≠0𝜆1¯𝜆2-1≠0, then we can say that v*w=0v*w=0. Let us assume that ⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1=0𝜆1¯𝜆2-1=0.
⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1=0
⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2=1
𝜆1⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2=𝜆1
|𝜆1|2𝜆2=𝜆1
(1)𝜆2=𝜆1
𝜆2=𝜆1
𝜆1¯𝜆2-1=0𝜆1¯𝜆2=1𝜆1𝜆1¯𝜆2=𝜆1|𝜆1|2𝜆2=𝜆1(1)𝜆2=𝜆1𝜆2=𝜆1The absolute value of the eigenvalues of a unitary matrix is 11. Therefore, |𝜆1|2=1|𝜆1|2=1. However, we see that if ⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1=0𝜆1¯𝜆2-1=0, then 𝜆1=𝜆2𝜆1=𝜆2. This contradicts the fact that 𝜆1≠𝜆2𝜆1≠𝜆2. Our assumption is wrong. ⏨⏨𝜆1𝜆2-1≠0𝜆1¯𝜆2-1≠0. Therefore, v*w=0v*w=0. Property 4 : Let U=U1U2U=U1U2, where U1U1 and U2U2 are unitary matrices. UU is also a unitary matrix. Proof :
U*U=(U1U2)*(U1U2)
=U*2U*1U1U2
=U*2IU2
=I
U*U=(U1U2)*(U1U2)=U2*U1*U1U2=U2*IU2=IProduct of two unitary matrices is also a unitary matrix. Orthogonal MatrixAn orthogonal matrix is a unitary matrix. Let Qn×nQn×n be an orthogonal matrix.• QTQ=I=QQTQTQ=I=QQT• The rows of an orthogonal matrix form an orthonormal set.• The columns of a orthogonal matrix form an orthonormal set.• ||Qv||2=||v||2||Qv||2=||v||2, for all v∈Rnv∈Rn• The absolute value of all the eigenvalues of QQ is 11• The eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of QQ are orthogonal.• Product of two orthogonal matrices is also an orthogonal matrix. Once again, the eigenvalues of QQ can be complex. Example,a
0-1
10
[0-110]The eigenvalues of the above matrix are ii and -i-i.

6. Schur's TheoremFor every square matrix A∈Cn×nA∈Cn×n, there exists a unitary matrix UU and an upper triangular matrix TT such that,A=UTU*A=UTU* Proof:Suppose, if A=a

7. Spectral TheoremLet AA be a hermitian matrix. Then, AA is unitarily diagonalizable.
A=UDU*
A=UDU*Here,• DD is a diagonal matrix, whose diagonal entries are the eigenvalues of AA• UU is a unitary matrix. The columns of UU are the eigenvectors of AA whose corresponding eigenvalues are the diagonal entries of DD. Proof : By Schur's theorem, there exists a unitary matrix QQ and an upper triangular matrix TT such that,
A=QTQ*
A*=(QTQ*)*
A*=QT*Q*
A=QTQ*A*=(QTQ*)*A*=QT*Q*Since AA is a hermitian matrix,
A*=A
QT*Q*=QTQ*
T*=T
A*=AQT*Q*=QTQ*T*=TSince TT is an upper traingular matrix, T*T* is a lower triangular matirx. An upper triangular matrix is equal to its conjugate transpose if and only if it is a diagonal matrix. Therefore, TT is a diagonal matrix. Real Symmetric Matrix & Orthogonally DiagonalizableA real symmetric matrix SS is also a hermitian matrix. Therefore, SS is untiarily diagonalizable.
S=QDQ*
S=QDQ*Here,• DD is a diagonal matrix, whose diagonal entries are the eigenvalues of SS• QQ is a unitary matrix. The columns of QQ are the eigenvectors of SS whose corresponding eigenvalues are the diagonal entries of DD. Since SS is a real symmetric matrix, we can further say that QQ is an orthogonal matrix. Proof : Let v≠0v≠0 be an eigenvector of Sn×nSn×n, i.e., Sv=𝜆vSv=𝜆v. Let a,b∈Rna,b∈Rn such that, v=a+ibv=a+ib.
Sv=𝜆v
S(a+ib)=𝜆(a+ib)
Sa+i(Sb)=𝜆a+i(𝜆b)
Sv=𝜆vS(a+ib)=𝜆(a+ib)Sa+i(Sb)=𝜆a+i(𝜆b)By equating the real and imaginary part, we get
Sa=𝜆aSb=𝜆b
Sa=𝜆aSb=𝜆baa and bb are also eigenvectors of SS with 𝜆𝜆 as eigenvalues. This means, for any eigenvalue of SS, we can a real eigenvector. Therefore, we can construct an orthogonal matrix QQ.
